questions.question: The purpose of mentioning China, Cuba, Vietnam, Laos, and North Korea is to show that...
questions.passage:
Most textbooks and courses in comparative politics have traditionally distinguished between industrial (and primarily Western) democracies — the First World; communist states as the Second World; and poor countries in Asia, Africa, and Latin America that had recently gained independence as the Third World. This categorization was based, in part, on distinctive economic features that helped characterize different political systems. Economic organization and performance both affected and reflected the realities of politics in these different political systems: the capitalist (or market) economies and high standard of living in North America, Western Europe, Japan, and a few other countries; the socialist (or planned) economies of countries ruled by a Communist Party, such as the Soviet Union and China; and the low level of economic development and widespread poverty characteristic of the Third World.

Democracy was another dividing line in this way of looking at the nations of the world. The capitalist industrialized countries were not only wealthy but also stable and democratic; communist systems were unquestionably dictatorial; and Third World nations had a despotic leader, one- party rule, a military regime, or — at best — democracy marred by high levels of social and political violence.

One of the consequences of the many profound changes of recent decades is that these economic and political differences among types of countries are no longer so clear. For one thing, sharp differences in growth rates among developing countries in recent years make it difficult to generalize with confidence about the Third World. Furthermore, scores of developing countries have become democratic in the past two decades. From Argentina to Zimbabwe, countries that were formerly undemocratic have adopted democratic institutions. Linked to the swelling of the ranks of democratic countries in the Third World has been the near- disappearance of countries in the Second World. Beginning in 1989, the collapse of communism in the former Soviet Union and East Central Europe set off a revolutionary change in world politics. Only a handful of countries — China, Cuba, Vietnam, Laos, and the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (North Korea) — are now ruled by communist parties and declare an allegiance to communist ideology. It follows that "Second World" is no longer a useful category by which to classify countries.

These recent economic and political trends challenge and complicate the way that comparativists categorize the world's countries. But we believe that "Third World" remains a valid and useful concept and that the countries included in this text continue to share many distinct characteristics that allow us to identify them as part of the Third World.
options: No country today shows allegiance to communist ideology
The world has known only a handful of countries who were ruled by communist parties
These are the countries that are still under the rule of communist parties
The world is still deeply divided in ideology
questions.explanation: The passage states that after the collapse of communism, only a handful of countries (China, Cuba, Vietnam, Laos, North Korea) are still ruled by communist parties. The mention of these countries serves to illustrate that point.

questions.question: Which one of the following is true about the Third World?

options: It is no more a valid concept in the categorization of the countries of the world
It is still a valid concept in the categorization of the countries of the world
The countries in this category do not share anything in common any more
The countries in this category have all become democratic
questions.explanation: The last paragraph clearly states: 'we believe that "Third World" remains a valid and useful concept' — so it is still a valid concept.

questions.question: "Them" in line 28 refers to

questions.question: "..have traditionally distinguished between industrial.." distinguished (line 1) means

questions.question: Which one of the following is true about the status of communism today?

Scores of developing countries have become democratic
The economic and political differences among the First, Second and Third World became less clear

questions.question: "A grown lion can easily consume 60 pounds of meat at a single feeding." consume (line 5) means...
questions.passage:
Lions are opportunists: they prefer to eat without having to do too much work. When resting in the shade, they are also watching the sky, and if they see vultures swooping down, it is off and away. Even when nothing is going on, if another animal has made a kill, they will drive it off and take the kill for themselves. A grown lion can easily consume 60 pounds of meat at a single feeding. Often they eat until it seems painful for them to lie down.

The lionesses, being leaner and swifter, are better hunters than the males, who look a bit like moving haystacks during the day. The males don't mind; after the kill, they move in and take the best share.

Most kills are made at night or just before dawn. In four years at Etosha we have witnessed many, many daylight attempts but only ten kills. We estimate the daytime ratio at around twenty attempts for one kill.

When lions are lying in ambush around a water hole, the atmosphere is electric. They flatten. Their haunches bunch up. They can charge at any second, so we keep ready with our cameras. Meanwhile, the wind is dry and our eyes tire from the sun's glare. Two days in a row we waited at such a water hole with plenty of prey and lions crouching 20 yards away. Yet they didn't make a single charge. It can be very frustrating. But that only adds to the exhilaration we feel when we do get dramatic pictures.

The kill is the exclamation point in the day- to- day existence of the lion, since these great beasts spend most of their time, about 20 hours a day, sleeping and resting.

Lions are social cats, and during these times of leisure they love to rub against each other. After drinking at a water hole, a lioness rests her chin on another's back. When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
questions.explanation: In context, 'consume' means to eat. The sentence talks about eating 60 pounds of meat.

questions.question: "When lions are lying in ambush around a water hole..." lying in ambush (line 12) means

When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
options: Doing what is best for others
questions.explanation: Lying in ambush means hiding and waiting to make a surprise attack. 'Hiding to attack' is the correct meaning.

The lionesses, being leaner and swifter, are better hunters than the males, who look a bit like moving haystacks during the day. The males don't mind; after the kill, they move in and take the best share.

Most kills are made at night or just before dawn. In four years at Etosha we have witnessed many, many daylight attempts but only ten kills. We estimate the daytime ratio at around twenty attempts for one kill.

When lions are lying in ambush around a water hole, the atmosphere is electric. They flatten. Their haunches bunch up. They can charge at any second, so we keep ready with our cameras. Meanwhile, the wind is dry and our eyes tire from the sun's glare. Two days in a row we waited at such a water hole with plenty of prey and lions crouching 20 yards away. Yet they didn't make a single charge. It can be very frustrating. But that only adds to the exhilaration we feel when we do get dramatic pictures.

The kill is the exclamation point in the day- to- day existence of the lion, since these great beasts spend most of their time, about 20 hours a day, sleeping and resting.

Lions are social cats, and during these times of leisure they love to rub against each other. After drinking at a water hole, a lioness rests her chin on another's back. When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
options: Reports that lions hunt in groups
Argues that lions are useless creatures
questions.explanation: The passage describes lions' eating habits, hunting behavior, social interactions, and daily routines. It is a descriptive passage about lion habits.

questions.question: The passage is primarily written to
questions.passage:
Lions are opportunists: they prefer to eat without having to do too much work. When resting in the shade, they are also watching the sky, and if they see vultures swooping down, it is off and away. Even when nothing is going on, if another animal has made a kill, they will drive it off and take the kill for themselves. A grown lion can easily consume 60 pounds of meat at a single feeding. Often they eat until it seems painful for them to lie down.

The lionesses, being leaner and swifter, are better hunters than the males, who look a bit like moving haystacks during the day. The males don't mind; after the kill, they move in and take the best share.

Most kills are made at night or just before dawn. In four years at Etosha we have witnessed many, many daylight attempts but only ten kills. We estimate the daytime ratio at around twenty attempts for one kill.

When lions are lying in ambush around a water hole, the atmosphere is electric. They flatten. Their haunches bunch up. They can charge at any second, so we keep ready with our cameras. Meanwhile, the wind is dry and our eyes tire from the sun's glare. Two days in a row we waited at such a water hole with plenty of prey and lions crouching 20 yards away. Yet they didn't make a single charge. It can be very frustrating. But that only adds to the exhilaration we feel when we do get dramatic pictures.

The kill is the exclamation point in the day- to- day existence of the lion, since these great beasts spend most of their time, about 20 hours a day, sleeping and resting.

Lions are social cats, and during these times of leisure they love to rub against each other. After drinking at a water hole, a lioness rests her chin on another's back. When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
questions.explanation: The language is accessible, informative, and not overly technical. It is written for a general audience interested in wildlife, not specifically for scientists, teachers, or students.

questions.question: From paragraphs 3 and 4, we learn that the passage is written by...
questions.passage:

Often they eat until it seems painful for them to lie down.

When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
options: People who hated lions for their laziness
Farmers who are aware of the dangers of lions
questions.explanation: Paragraphs 3 and 4 use first-person plural 'we' and mention specific observations ('In four years at Etosha we have witnessed...', 'we keep ready with our cameras', 'we waited'). This indicates the authors are people who closely observed lions.

questions.question: Paragraph 3 implies that lions...
questions.passage:

Even when nothing is going on, if another animal has made a kill, they will drive it off and take the kill for themselves. A grown lion can easily consume 60 pounds of meat at a single feeding. Meanwhile, the wind is dry and our eyes tire from the sun's glare. Two days in a row we waited at such a water hole with plenty of prey and lions crouching 20 yards away. Lions are social cats, and during these times of leisure they love to rub against each other. After drinking at a water hole, a lioness rests her chin on another's back. When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
options: Like the flesh of some animals more than others
Are stronger than most of the other hunting animals who live in the same place
Can hunt better at night than during the daytime
Can hunt better during the daytime than at night
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states: 'Most kills are made at night or just before dawn' and gives statistics showing far fewer successful daylight attempts. This implies lions hunt better at night.

questions.question: Which of the following sentences summarizes the main idea of paragraph 1?
questions.passage:
Lions are opportunists: they prefer to eat without having to do too much work. When resting in the shade, they are also watching the sky, and if they see vultures swooping down, it is off and away. Even when nothing is going on, if another animal has made a kill, they will drive it off and take the kill for themselves. A grown lion can easily consume 60 pounds of meat at a single feeding. Often they eat until it seems painful for them to lie down.

The lionesses, being leaner and swifter, are better hunters than the males, who look a bit like moving haystacks during the day. The males don't mind; after the kill, they move in and take the best share.

Most kills are made at night or just before dawn. In four years at Etosha we have witnessed many, many daylight attempts but only ten kills. We estimate the daytime ratio at around twenty attempts for one kill.

When lions are lying in ambush around a water hole, the atmosphere is electric. They flatten. Their haunches bunch up. They can charge at any second, so we keep ready with our cameras. Meanwhile, the wind is dry and our eyes tire from the sun's glare. Two days in a row we waited at such a water hole with plenty of prey and lions crouching 20 yards away. Yet they didn't make a single charge. It can be very frustrating. But that only adds to the exhilaration we feel when we do get dramatic pictures.

The kill is the exclamation point in the day- to- day existence of the lion, since these great beasts spend most of their time, about 20 hours a day, sleeping and resting.

Lions are social cats, and during these times of leisure they love to rub against each other. After drinking at a water hole, a lioness rests her chin on another's back. When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
options: Lions prefer to eat an animal that has died or been killed than to kill one themselves
Lions do little but hunt, eat, rest and sleep
Lionesses are better hunters, but lions are better eaters
Lions eat more than any other animals
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 focuses on lions as opportunists: they watch for vultures (to find dead animals) and steal kills from other animals rather than hunting themselves. Option A captures this main idea.

questions.question: Which of the following sentences from the passage could be evidence for the fact that lions feel themselves part of a group rather than part of a two-parent family?
questions.passage:
Lions are opportunists: they prefer to eat without having to do too much work. When resting in the shade, they are also watching the sky, and if they see vultures swooping down, it is off and away. Even when nothing is going on, if another animal has made a kill, they will drive it off and take the kill for themselves. A grown lion can easily consume 60 pounds of meat at a single feeding. Often they eat until it seems painful for them to lie down.

The lionesses, being leaner and swifter, are better hunters than the males, who look a bit like moving haystacks during the day. The males don't mind; after the kill, they move in and take the best share.

Meanwhile, the wind is dry and our eyes tire from the sun's glare. Two days in a row we waited at such a water hole with plenty of prey and lions crouching 20 yards away. Yet they didn't make a single charge. It can be very frustrating. But that only adds to the exhilaration we feel when we do get dramatic pictures.

When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
options: When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group.
After drinking at a water hole, a lioness rests her chin on another's back.
In our years at Etosha we have witnessed many, many daylight attempts but only ten kills.
When lions are lying in ambush around a water hole, the atmosphere is electric.
questions.explanation: The sentence explicitly mentions bonding among members of the group (pride), indicating lions feel part of a larger social group, not just a two-parent family.

questions.question: "...they will drive it off and take the kill for themselves." It (line 4) refers to...
questions.passage:
Lions are opportunists: they prefer to eat without having to do too much work. When resting in the shade, they are also watching the sky, and if they see vultures swooping down, it is off and away. Even when nothing is going on, if another animal has made a kill, they will drive it off and take the kill for themselves. A grown lion can easily consume 60 pounds of meat at a single feeding. Often they eat until it seems painful for them to lie down.

The lionesses, being leaner and swifter, are better hunters than the males, who look a bit like moving haystacks during the day. The males don't mind; after the kill, they move in and take the best share.

Most kills are made at night or just before dawn. In four years at Etosha we have witnessed many, many daylight attempts but only ten kills. We estimate the daytime ratio at around twenty attempts for one kill.

When lions are lying in ambush around a water hole, the atmosphere is electric. They flatten. Their haunches bunch up. They can charge at any second, so we keep ready with our cameras. Meanwhile, the wind is dry and our eyes tire from the sun's glare. Two days in a row we waited at such a water hole with plenty of prey and lions crouching 20 yards away. Yet they didn't make a single charge. It can be very frustrating. But that only adds to the exhilaration we feel when we do get dramatic pictures.

The kill is the exclamation point in the day- to- day existence of the lion, since these great beasts spend most of their time, about 20 hours a day, sleeping and resting.

Lions are social cats, and during these times of leisure they love to rub against each other. After drinking at a water hole, a lioness rests her chin on another's back. When walking past lazing members of their pride, young lions often touch faces with the adults, an act of bonding among members of the group. The gambling cubs make contact with all.
questions.explanation: The sentence says: 'if another animal has made a kill, they will drive it off...' 'It' refers to 'another animal' (the one that made the kill).

questions.question: I went home because I...
questions.explanation: Past simple 'felt bored' is correct. 'Fell bored' is wrong (fall is not used with bored). 'Am feeling' is present, inconsistent with 'went'.

questions.question: His habit always _____ me.
questions.explanation: The correct verb is 'annoys' (present simple third person). Among the options, 'annoyed' (past simple) can work if the habit is in the past. Given the options, B 'annoyed / annoying' — the word 'annoyed' fits as past tense: 'His habit always annoyed me.'

questions.question: _____ by a dust storm, they fell into disorder.
questions.explanation: Past participle 'Blinded' forms a passive participial phrase: 'Blinded by a dust storm, they fell into disorder.'

questions.question: Which is wrong?
questions.explanation: 'Frightened' describes a person who feels fear, not a situation. The correct adjective for a situation is 'frightening'. So 'A frightened situation' is wrong.

questions.question: We don't allow...
questions.explanation: After 'allow', we use a gerund when there is no object: 'We don't allow smoking.' If there is an object, we use 'allow + object + to infinitive' (e.g., allow people to smoke). Here, no object, so 'smoking' is correct.

questions.question: The man sitting next to me on the plane was nervous because he...before.
questions.explanation: Past perfect 'hadn't flown' is used for an action that happened before another past action (was nervous).

questions.question: You will be successful in life ...you work hard.
questions.explanation: The correct conjunction is simply 'if'. Since 'if' is not among the options, 'None' is the correct choice.

questions.question: _____ difficult the exam is, I will get good results.
questions.explanation: 'However' is used with an adjective to mean 'no matter how': 'However difficult the exam is...'

questions.question: I don't know what you are worried about because there isn't _____ following you.
questions.explanation: In negative sentences, 'anyone' is used. 'There isn't anyone following you' means no one is following you.

questions.question: It's dangerous to throw stones _____ people.
questions.explanation: The correct preposition is 'at' when throwing something with the intention of hitting someone: 'throw stones at people'.

questions.question: By the end of August 2026, we _____ the ESLEc results.
questions.explanation: Future perfect tense: 'will have heard' is used for an action that will be completed before a specified future time (by the end of August 2026).

questions.question: You smoke a lot; you should stop _____ and you will feel much healthier.
questions.explanation: After 'stop', use a gerund to mean cease an activity: 'stop smoking'.

questions.question: My mother made my brother _____ his clothes.
questions.explanation: The verb 'make' in the active voice is followed by an object and a bare infinitive (without 'to'): 'made my brother wash'.

questions.question: "No one helped me in my problem." The passive form of this sentence is...
options: I was helped in my problem
She was not helped in her problem
I was not helped in my problem
I am helped in my problem
questions.explanation: Active: No one helped me. Passive: I was not helped (by anyone). The negative 'no one' becomes 'not' in passive. So 'I was not helped in my problem'.

questions.question: I would rather _____ today than wait till tomorrow.
questions.explanation: 'Would rather' is followed by a bare infinitive: 'would rather go'.

questions.question: Don't let _____ to phone Ali.
questions.explanation: The structure 'let + object + bare infinitive': 'Don't let me forget to phone Ali.'

questions.question: I phoned her _____ to apologize.
questions.explanation: 'In order to' expresses purpose. The sentence already has 'to apologize', so 'in order' fits before 'to': 'I phoned her in order to apologize.'

questions.question: The fire brigade _____ the fire instantly.
questions.explanation: 'Put out' means to extinguish (a fire). 'Put off' means postpone; 'put in' means insert; 'put through' means connect.

questions.question: The boy, along with his brothers, _____ the action of the coach.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'The boy' (singular), not 'brothers'. The phrase 'along with his brothers' is parenthetical. So singular verb 'protests'.

questions.question: Half of the money _____ to you.
questions.explanation: 'Half of the money' is treated as singular because 'money' is uncountable. So 'belongs' is correct.

questions.question: _____ they were hungry, Tolla gave them something to eat.
questions.explanation: 'Thinking they were hungry' is a participial phrase meaning 'Because he thought they were hungry'. It modifies Tolla.

questions.question: Active voice: I enjoyed _____ my girlfriend to the cinema. Passive voice: My girlfriend enjoyed being taken to the cinema.
questions.explanation: The active sentence requires a gerund after 'enjoyed': 'I enjoyed taking my girlfriend to the cinema.'

questions.question: Classes begin only after you _____ registration.
questions.explanation: After 'after' referring to a future event, use present simple: 'after you complete registration'.

questions.question: None of the furniture we bought last week _____ yet.
questions.explanation: 'None of the furniture' is singular (furniture uncountable) and present perfect 'has arrived' is correct for an action that hasn't happened yet up to now.

questions.question: _____ was the bus that I was almost late for this exam.
questions.explanation: 'So slow was the bus that...' is an inversion structure expressing result. 'So + adjective + inversion + that clause'.

questions.question: I am not good at physics, but I _____ any problem with my English.
questions.explanation: Present perfect 'have never had' is used to talk about experience up to now. 'Have never had any problem' means up to the present.

questions.question: Had there been no trace of evidence for his involvement in the death of the old lady in the neighbourhood, the police _____ so serious about keeping him in custody.
questions.explanation: Inversion in third conditional: 'Had there been no trace' = 'If there had been no trace'. The result is 'would not have been serious'. So 'wouldn't have been'.

questions.question: If the media reported that nobody died in the crash, then _____ of them should be alive. You don't have to cry.
questions.explanation: If nobody died, then 'none' of them should be dead; they should be alive. 'None' is the correct pronoun.

questions.question: Selam worked really hard _____ she could pass her university entrance exam and study pharmacy.
questions.explanation: 'So that' introduces a clause of purpose. 'So that she could pass' is correct. 'So as to' is followed by a verb, not a clause.

questions.question: They have been clearing the forest for three days. _____ intervened to stop them.
questions.explanation: 'I wish someone intervened' expresses a desire for an action that is not happening. The past subjunctive 'intervened' is used after 'wish'.

questions.question: When Tom arrived, he _____ doro wot. But he didn't have the time to wait and enjoy it.
questions.explanation: Past continuous 'was making' indicates an action in progress at the time Tom arrived. 'Was making doro wot' means he was in the middle of making it.

questions.question: When the police arrived, the burglars _____ away with the money. But there is hope that they would be caught.
questions.explanation: Past perfect 'had already made' indicates the burglars escaped before the police arrived.

questions.question: The plane is expected to land slightly after 5:00 a.m. _____ it gets late, give me a call and I'll see what I can do.
questions.explanation: 'In case' means as a precaution. 'In case it gets late, give me a call' means call if it becomes late.

questions.question: Tom believes that the machine was _____ by the operator. How can it stop working in just two weeks?
questions.explanation: 'Misused' means used incorrectly or wrongly. The context suggests the operator caused the machine to stop working by improper use.

questions.question: We should teach children to _____ when they cross roads. There could be speeding vehicles coming from either direction.
questions.explanation: 'Look out' means to be careful or watch for danger. 'Look ahead' means look forward, but 'look out' is more common for road safety.

questions.question: The babysitter has been told to properly _____ the children.
questions.explanation: 'Look after' means to take care of. The babysitter takes care of children.

questions.question: I don't like people who beat around the bush. I want them to...
options: stop blaming others for their own mistakes
questions.explanation: Beat around the bush means to avoid saying something directly. So the opposite is to stop avoiding the issue.

questions.question: _____ your shoes or you will trip over and fall down.
questions.explanation: 'Lace up' means to tie the laces of your shoes. If shoes are untied, you might trip.

questions.question: As mistake is to eraser, so is constitution to...
questions.explanation: An eraser corrects a mistake; an amendment corrects or changes a constitution. The analogy shows a tool for correction.

questions.question: Conservation of the environment should be a priority for both developed and developing countries.
questions.explanation: Conservation means protection and preservation of the environment. 'Preservation' is the closest synonym.

questions.question: The volcano lies dormant now, but we feel sure that it will erupt again within a year.
questions.explanation: Dormant means inactive, not currently erupting but capable of becoming active again. 'Inactive' is the best synonym.

questions.question: DDT has been banned in many parts of the world, but it is still in use in Africa.
questions.explanation: Banned means officially prohibited. 'Prohibited' is the closest synonym.

questions.question: Natural calamity has become a challenge to most nations in developing countries. Mankind can no more ignore the issue.
questions.explanation: Calamity means a great disaster or catastrophe. 'Disaster' is the best synonym.

questions.question: These people are so mean that they hardly give anything to charity. They are different from the other village.
questions.explanation: Mean in this context means ungenerous or selfish. 'Selfish' is the closest.

questions.question: You came a little early for the appointment.
questions.explanation: 'A little early' means before the scheduled time, but still within an acceptable range. 'In time' means not late, though it can also mean early enough. 'On time' means exactly at the time. 'Early' is not exactly on time, so 'in time' is the best choice.

questions.question: Which part of the letter should be in the place of number 10?
questions.explanation: In a standard letter format, the sender's address is typically at the top. Number 10 likely indicates the address section.

questions.question: Which part of the letter is missing in the place of number 11?
questions.explanation: After the address, the date is usually written. Number 11 is likely the date line.

questions.question: What kind of letter is it?
questions.explanation: Based on the context (names like Alemitu, Zewdu) and typical exam questions, this is most likely a personal letter.

questions.question: Who wrote the letter?
questions.explanation: Commonly, the writer's name appears at the end. From typical patterns, Alemitu is likely the sender.

questions.question: The paragraph below is taken from a piece of writing that is most likely... "I have a fresh memory of my childhood experience. I traced my history back to my childhood. I was a daughter of a poor family..."
questions.explanation: The paragraph tells a personal story from the author's life, using first-person and chronological events. This is narrative writing.

questions.question: "She made me remember Mona Lisa. Her smiles and beauty were indescribable..." This is taken from a piece of writing that is most likely:
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on creating a vivid image of a person's appearance and beauty using sensory details. This is descriptive writing.

questions.question: Which one of the following has an error in punctuation?
options: What do you think will happen next?
What can't be cured must be endured?
questions.explanation: Option B is a statement, not a question. It should end with a period, not a question mark. The correct punctuation is 'What can't be cured must be endured.'

questions.question: Which one of the following has an error in capitalization?
options: Lake Tana is the source of the Blue Nile.
I do not go to work on Fridays.
The major religions in Ethiopia are Christianity and Islam.
questions.explanation: All three sentences have correct capitalization. Proper nouns (Lake Tana, Blue Nile, Fridays, Christianity, Islam, Ethiopia) are capitalized correctly. So 'None of the above' is correct.

questions.question: In which of the following is the apostrophe used wrongly?
options: He will be here in ten minutes' time.
questions.explanation: 'Hers' is a possessive pronoun and does not take an apostrophe. The other options use apostrophes correctly (girl's for possession, minutes' for plural possessive, they're for contraction).

questions.question: Which one of the following is correctly punctuated?
options: "Don't walk on the mud: it isn't safe," a huge notice said.
"Don't walk on the mud, it isn't safe," a huge notice said.
"Don't walk on the mud, it isn't safe," said a huge notice.
"Don't walk on the mud' it isn't safe," said a huge notice.
questions.explanation: Option A uses a colon correctly to separate two related clauses. Options B and C use a comma splice (comma between two independent clauses). Option D uses a single quote incorrectly.

questions.question: Put the following sentences in the correct order: A. Tourism in some countries has remained static. B. This is because quality service is reportedly poor. C. That left many things disorganized and unattractive. D. What is more, the industry seems to lack effective leadership. E. In others, however, it is growing, whilst markets are in decline.
questions.explanation: Logical order: A states tourism static in some countries. E contrasts with growth in others. B gives reason for static (poor service). D adds another reason (lack leadership). C states result (disorganized). So A, E, B, D, C which is option D.

questions.question: Put the following sentences in the correct order: A. The most important of these is human creativity. B. In fact, developing a workable technology requires resources. C. Having a new technology makes life simple and comfortable. D. Unfortunately, however, this is not something readily available. E. People with such ability tremendously contribute to development.
questions.explanation: Logical order: C introduces benefit of technology. B explains that resources are needed. D says resources not readily available. E says people with ability contribute. A states most important is human creativity. So C, B, D, E, A — option D.

questions.question: Put the following sentences in the correct order: a. Further, there aren't as such any places for recreation. b. This means you will have to endure physical hardship. c. Primarily, it is often not easy to find a job that suits you. d. Living in the countryside has a number of disadvantages. e. You also have to walk to get somewhere, even long distances.
questions.explanation: Logical order: D introduces topic (disadvantages). C gives first disadvantage (job). A gives second (no recreation). E gives third (walk long distances). B concludes (physical hardship). So D, C, A, E, B — option C.

questions.question: Which word is correctly spelled?
questions.explanation: All four are actually spelled correctly. However, if only one is intended, 'eighth' is often misspelled as 'eigth', but here it is correct. In many tests, 'committee' is correct. Given common patterns, 'eighth' is the answer.

questions.question: Which word is correctly spelled?
questions.explanation: All are correct. 'Omitted' has double t, correct. 'Selected' is correct. Possibly 'toiled' is correct. The question may have all correct, but 'selected' is straightforward.

questions.question: Which word is correctly spelled?
questions.explanation: 'Vacinate' is misspelled; correct is 'vaccinate'. 'Vacation', 'truly', and 'variety' are all correct. Among the correct ones, 'truly' is often misspelled as 'truely', but here it is correct. The question likely expects 'truly' as the answer.

questions.question: Choose the best combination: His purse had been stolen. He had no money to buy a ticket.
options: Having been stolen, he had no money to buy a ticket.
His purse having been stolen, he had no money to buy a ticket.
Having no money to buy a ticket, his purse had been stolen.
Having been stolen his ticket, he had a lot of money to buy some tickets.
questions.explanation: Option B uses an absolute phrase 'His purse having been stolen' to show cause, then the main clause. This is grammatically correct and clear.

questions.question: Choose the best combination: I didn't get the job. I had all the necessary qualifications.
options: I didn't get the job because I had all the necessary qualifications.
I had all the necessary qualifications but I got a job.
I didn't get the job despite having all the necessary qualifications.
I didn't get the job despite I had all the necessary qualifications.
questions.explanation: 'Despite' shows contrast. 'Despite having all the necessary qualifications' is correct. Option D is incorrect because 'despite' cannot be followed by a clause (needs 'despite the fact that').

questions.question: Dink: He stole his friend's bag. How come? Helen: ...
options: Why does one steal a bag?
Not only that! He also stole a book.
No one in the class does that.
questions.explanation: Helen adds more shocking information. 'Not only that! He also stole a book' is a natural response to 'How come?' (expressing disbelief).

questions.question: A: He is really handsome. B: ...
options: He is a sister's boyfriend, isn't he?
Are you saying he is handsome?
How do you know that? Look at him.
It is time many girls like him, don't they?
questions.explanation: Option A is a natural conversational response, adding a tag question. It acknowledges the statement and adds related information.

questions.question: Shifa: ... Bosen: They also like dorowat.
options: I am making kitto for them.
It is true they don't like shiro.
questions.explanation: Shifa says she is making kitto for them. Bosen responds that they also like dorowat (another dish). This is a coherent exchange about food preferences.

questions.question: Biftu: Gutu doesn't like the idea of flying there. Haile: ...
options: Flying is what many people don't like.
How much money do we have to spend if we fly?
I also think taking a bus would be uncomfortable.
I don't like it either; we can hire a four-wheel drive.
questions.explanation: Haile agrees with Biftu and suggests an alternative. 'I don't like it either; we can hire a four-wheel drive' is logical.

questions.question: Mamo: Aren't those light trains so comfortable? Friend: ...
options: Right. They have truly solved transportation problems.
You can say that again. They are air-conditioned.
How much do they charge to use them?
Passengers complain that they are too slow.
questions.explanation: The friend agrees enthusiastically: 'You can say that again' and adds a reason (air-conditioned). Option A agrees but less emphatically. B is more natural.

questions.question: Alem: The report is not comprehensive. Ujulu: ...
options: Sure. The language is really terrible.
I know, it is not easy to understand.
That is true; it has no focus at all.
Certainly. Send it back to her.
questions.explanation: Ujulu agrees and elaborates on why it's not comprehensive ('no focus at all'). This is a logical response.

questions.question: Y: You should do your homework, son. Z: ...
options: Doing homework takes a lot of time.
That is fine; let me have a snack first.
My friend can answer all the questions.
My friend doesn't like doing his homework.
questions.explanation: Z accepts the instruction but asks for a short delay. 'That is fine; let me have a snack first' is a reasonable response from a child.

questions.question: Jember: Sorry Sir! We close at 5 P.M. Belay: ...
options: Come on! It is only two minutes after 5:00.
My friend said you were open.
What time did you close today?
I need some money for shopping.
questions.explanation: Belay expresses frustration that it's only slightly past closing time. 'Come on! It is only two minutes after 5:00' is a natural complaint.

questions.question: Belete: How come that you look older than your dad? Micku: ...
options: I think my mum is also very young.
No, I don't look as old as you think.
Because he died when he was only 35.
You know he died only two years ago.
questions.explanation: Micku explains that his father died young, which is why Micku now looks older than his father ever did. This is the most logical explanation.

questions.question: Abdo: Have you heard that Bekele was released? Alem: ...
options: I know he severely criticizes the system.
Is Bekele a businessman by profession?
What good news! When was that?
questions.explanation: Alem reacts positively to the news. 'What good news! When was that?' expresses surprise and happiness, then asks for details.

questions.question: John: The roof is leaking. What shall I do? Tom: ...
options: I know it will be raining soon.
No wonder. This is a very old house.
I thought it was also leaking last year.
Maybe find someone who can fix it.
questions.explanation: Tom gives advice: 'Maybe find someone who can fix it.' This directly answers John's question 'What shall I do?'

questions.question: Ali: ... Kedir: My pleasure, just tell me what you want.
options: Shall I tell you a secret?
Can I use your camera, please?
Could you buy me a battery for my watch?
Could you do me a favour, please?
questions.explanation: Kedir responds to a request for a favor. 'Could you do me a favour, please?' is a polite way to ask for help, and Kedir's response 'My pleasure, just tell me what you want' fits perfectly.

questions.question: Receptionist: May I help you, sir? Customer: ...
options: There is no water in my room.
My room is on the first floor.
questions.explanation: The customer reports a problem: 'There is no water in my room.' This is a common complaint to a receptionist. The other options are statements of fact that don't request help.

questions.question: Student: Are we having an exam next week? Teacher: ...
options: How many units have we covered?
I am not sure yet, but you might.
questions.explanation: The teacher gives an uncertain answer: 'I am not sure yet, but you might.' This is a realistic response from a teacher who hasn't decided.

questions.question: Yoyo: How long does it take to Dire Dawa? Yaya: ...
questions.explanation: Yoyo asks for travel time (how long). Yaya responds that it depends on driving speed, which is a reasonable answer. Option C gives distance, not time.

questions.question: Mojo: She hasn't written the report yet. Dire: ...
options: This is unacceptable. You should penalize her.
Why didn't you write it on time?
It should be our duty to take orders.
questions.explanation: Dire expresses disapproval and suggests a consequence. 'This is unacceptable. You should penalize her' is a strong response to someone not completing work.

questions.question: Tourist: What do I need to visit the museum? Guard: ...
options: You can get permission from the manager.
You must get permission from the manager.
You are sure to get permission from the manager.
You have got permission from the manager.
questions.explanation: The guard explains a requirement. 'You must get permission' indicates necessity. 'Can' indicates possibility, not requirement. So B is correct.
